reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Self-contained opening paragraph with concrete pricing immediately answers 'what is this and why does it matter' — AEO-optimized and extractable
FeatureComparisonTable with ALB is the most important decision support on the page and is positioned exactly where readers need it
Catch-all routing pattern example is realistic and directly covers the most common real-world use case (Hono, Express, FastAPI apps on a single Lambda)
CORS section explicitly addresses when to skip CORS entirely, not just how to configure it — good decision-support discipline
CDN section contains a concrete 'When to skip CDN' paragraph with reasoning tied to API dynamism, not just feature description
FAQ has exactly 10 Q&A pairs with strong category mix: Stacktape-specific, AWS-specific (payload limits, cold starts), and comparison questions (ALB vs HTTP API, REST vs HTTP)
All code examples use the correct class-based defineConfig pattern with valid class names verified against the source types
Routing table (exact / parameterized / greedy) is a high-signal reference that readers will return to repeatedly
problems: FACTUAL ERROR in Alarms section: the prose implies `statistic` applies to both alarm types ('The `statistic` property controls aggregation... Both alarm types support a custom `comparisonOperator`'). Per source, HttpApiGatewayErrorRateTriggerProps extends only TriggerWithCustomComparison — it does NOT extend TriggerWithCustomStatFunction. The `statistic` property exists only on the latency alarm.

Secondary code examples (CORS, CDN, access logging, payload format, alarms) show the import line fully visible outside any focus markers. The style guide explicitly lists this as a forbidden form: imports must be hidden from rendered snippets using focus markers.

Route authorization section states 'Stacktape supports two authorizer types — Cognito (JWT-based) and Lambda (custom logic)' — StpAuthorizer is not defined in the provided source files, making this claim unverifiable against source.

payloadFormat default described as '1.0' at gateway level, but the source only documents this default in HttpApiIntegrationProps (integration level), not in HttpApiGatewayProps — the gateway-level field has no @default annotation in the provided types.

retentionDays described as 'supported values range from 1 to 3653' — the source defines a discrete union (1 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 14 | 30 | 60 | 90 | 120 | 150 | 180 | 365 | 400 | 545 | 731 | 1827 | 3653), not a continuous range. Arbitrary values like 45 are not valid.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the factual error in the Alarms section: the `statistic` property belongs only to the latency alarm (HttpApiGatewayLatencyTriggerProps extends TriggerWithCustomStatFunction), NOT the error rate alarm (HttpApiGatewayErrorRateTriggerProps extends only TriggerWithCustomComparison). Rewrite to make this explicit: 'For the latency alarm, `statistic` controls how the metric is aggregated — `avg` (default), `p90`, `p95`, `p99`, `min`, `max`, or `sum`. Both alarm types support `comparisonOperator` (default: GreaterThanThreshold).'
optionalImprovements: Extend focus markers in secondary examples (CORS, CDN, logging, payload format, alarms) to start before `export default defineConfig` so the import is outside or dimmed — e.g., place `// [!code focus-start]` before the `export default` line and `// [!code focus-end]` at the closing brace of the return statement, then add inline `// [!code focus]` on just the property being highlighted

Narrow the route authorization claim: replace the specific Cognito/Lambda enumeration with a forward reference — 'Routes can require authentication via authorizers configured on each integration. See HTTP triggers for authorizer options' — since StpAuthorizer's variants are not in the provided source
Fix retentionDays wording: 'specific values: 1, 3, 5, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 365, 400, 545, 731, 1827, or 3653 days' instead of 'range from 1 to 3653'

Either add a one-line explanation of Route53 hosted zone for AWS novices ('Route53 is AWS's DNS service; a hosted zone holds your domain's DNS records — set one up with stacktape domain:add') or leave the link to domain:add doing that work, but remove the bare phrase 'Route53 hosted zone' from the prose without explanation

The introduction mentions 'built-in throttling' but no section covers it — either add a brief note (default account-level API Gateway throttling limits apply) or remove the claim from the intro to avoid reader confusion when they search the page for throttling details

Consider softening the payloadFormat default claim to 'The default at the integration level is 1.0; setting it at the gateway level provides a fallback for all routes' to avoid implying HttpApiGatewayProps has an explicit @default

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with TypeScript class-based resources — no forbidden YAML blocks or plain-object StacktapeConfig patterns.
Routing section clearly documents all three path pattern types (exact, parameterized, greedy) in a scannable table anchored directly to the source's HttpApiIntegrationProps.path documentation.
Feature comparison table between HTTP API Gateway and ALB is concrete and decision-useful — covers pricing model, scaling, routing options, and built-in JWT auth.
Alarm section accurately covers both HttpApiGatewayErrorRateTrigger and HttpApiGatewayLatencyTrigger with correct type strings, threshold properties, and statistic enum values from source.
CORS section correctly notes that gateway-level settings override application-code CORS headers, and accurately reflects the source's allowedMethods auto-detection behavior.
FAQ has exactly 10 Q&A pairs spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS service use-case, and comparison/choosing questions.
ApiReference uses definitionName='HttpApiGatewayProps' which matches the interface name in the source types exactly.
When to use / When NOT to use are correctly structured as peer H2 sections, not nested.
CDN section provides concrete decision guidance (when to enable, when to skip) with cost/tradeoff context and the cloudfrontPriceClass property values explained.
problems: FACTUAL INACCURACY (production impact): The logging section states 'supported values range from 1 to 3653 days (10 years)' for retentionDays. The source type is a discrete union: 1 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 14 | 30 | 60 | 90 | 120 | 150 | 180 | 365 | 400 | 545 | 731 | 1827 | 3653. The word 'range' implies any integer is accepted. A production engineer who sets retentionDays: 45 or retentionDays: 200 will get a type/validation error — the documentation would directly mislead them.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the retentionDays description in the Access logging section. Replace 'supported values range from 1 to 3653 days (10 years)' with a statement reflecting the discrete allowed values from the source union type. For example: 'retentionDays must be one of the allowed values: 1, 3, 5, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 365, 400, 545, 731, 1827, or 3653 days.' Continuous-range language will mislead engineers who want a non-standard retention like 45 or 200 days.
optionalImprovements: The CORS section mentions allowedHeaders in passing but doesn't explain when it's needed. A one-sentence note that allowedHeaders must list any custom request headers (e.g., Authorization, X-Api-Key) your frontend sends would save readers a debugging round-trip.

The Access logging section could briefly mention that the log format (CLF, XML, CSV) matters mostly for external tooling integration, and that JSON is the best choice for forwarding to Datadog/OpenSearch/Highlight — this pairs naturally with the log-forwarding link already present.

The CDN section links to 'CDN' for the full configuration reference but doesn't state whether the CDN's domain is separate from the API Gateway's custom domain — this is a common point of confusion when both customDomains and cdn are configured together.

Consider adding a note in the 'When NOT to use' section that HTTP API Gateway also has a per-route timeout maximum (from source: API Gateway has a 29-second maximum integration timeout) since Lambda functions with long-running operations will silently time out at the gateway before the function finishes.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: First paragraph is 60 words, self-contained, answers 'what is this and why does it matter' without meta-framing — strong AEO snippet.
FeatureComparisonTable for HTTP API Gateway vs ALB covers all comparison axes (pricing, routing, scaling-to-zero, auth, CDN) with concrete values — tables are cited 2-4x more often.
Property-value anchors are present throughout: cloudfrontPriceClass alternatives named, logging format options listed, statistic options enumerated, retentionDays range stated.
Decision support is present in every opt-in section (CDN, CORS, custom domains): each has a when-to-enable, when-to-skip, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation.

FAQ has exactly 10 questions spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (routes, custom domains, CORS, WAF), AWS use-case (cost, payload limit, cold-start, REST vs HTTP API), and choosing/comparison (HTTP API vs ALB, WebSocket). FAQ answers are 2-4 sentences and self-contained.
Routing table (exact / path-parameter / greedy) is a high-signal extraction target — concrete examples in each cell.

All alarm types from the source union (error-rate, latency) are covered with a working example, per-alarm explanation of threshold semantics, and statistic/comparisonOperator options sourced from types.
Code examples are complete and copyable — no `// fill in` placeholders. The basic example shows a full working gateway + Lambda integration.
problems: Mandatory style violation: visible `import { ... } from 'stacktape'` lines in six secondary config examples (CORS, custom domains, CDN, access logging, payload format, alarms). In those examples, `[!code focus-start]` / `[!code focus-end]` markers are placed only around the specific property being configured (e.g., `cors: { ... }`), leaving the import line and the `export default defineConfig(...)` wrapper outside the focus region — visible (or dimmed but readable) in the rendered output. The two examples that place focus markers around the entire `export default defineConfig(() => { ... })` block (basic and catch-all) correctly hide the import.

The authorizer subsection ('Route authorization') states Stacktape supports 'Cognito (JWT-based) and Lambda (custom logic)' authorizer types. The provided source files only expose `authorizer?: StpAuthorizer` in `HttpApiIntegrationProps` — the concrete union breakdown is not in the supplied source. The claim is reasonable but technically ungrounded for a resource page (the reference to [HTTP triggers] mitigates this).

Minor: The FAQ answer for 'Can I protect my API Gateway with a firewall?' asserts 'AWS WAF does not support API Gateway V2 HTTP APIs.' This is a well-known AWS fact and allowed for FAQ, but it contradicts none of the source — no issue with penalty, just a note for fact-checking if AWS expands WAF support.
mandatoryFixes: Fix visible import lines in all six secondary config examples. Restructure each example so `[!code focus-start]` appears before `export default defineConfig(` (or before the `import` line) and `[!code focus-end]` closes after the return statement, mirroring the pattern used in the basic and catch-all examples. This hides the import from the rendered output while keeping it present for type-checking. Affected examples: CORS, Custom domains, CDN, Access logging, Payload format, Alarms. Penalty applied: scannability -1.
optionalImprovements: The 'Route authorization' subsection cites two authorizer types without code examples. If the HTTP triggers page covers this in depth, a single cross-link sentence is fine — but if this page is the primary landing point for auth decisions, a minimal Cognito-authorizer snippet would strengthen it.

The `disabledGlobalAlarms` property is mentioned only in passing at the end of the alarms section. One sentence explaining where global alarms are configured (Stacktape Console) would help the reader understand what they are opting out of.

The catch-all routing pattern note ('This is the most common pattern for building APIs with Stacktape') is a strong opinionated claim — it could be lightly qualified ('for framework-based APIs like Hono or Express') to be more precise without weakening the recommendation.

The 'Access logging' section links to log-forwarding but does not name any concrete integration examples (Datadog, Highlight.io are mentioned). Adding 'See Log forwarding for setup details' with a brief hint about which fields to key on (e.g., JSON format is required for most forwarders) would add actionability.

verifierResults.summary: The HTTP API Gateway draft is well-grounded overall. Most claims — pricing, CORS defaults, alarm types, payload format options, referenceable params, and routing behavior — align closely with the source types. I found one medium-severity factual issue (retentionDays described as a continuous range when the source defines discrete allowed values) and one low-severity ambiguity (authorizer types not verifiable from provided sources). Code examples use correct class names, proper CodeBlock format, and valid property names. Links all match the navigation index. FAQ covers the required three categories with 10 items.

issues.statement: The `retentionDays` property defaults to `30`; supported values range from `1` to `3653` days (10 years).
issues.reason: The source defines `retentionDays` as a union of specific discrete values, not a continuous range. Saying 'range from 1 to 3653' implies any integer in that span is valid, which would cause validation errors for values like 2, 10, 20, 45, 100, etc.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace 'supported values range from 1 to 3653 days (10 years)' with the actual allowed values or a representative sample: 'Allowed values: 1, 3, 5, 7, 14, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, 365, 400, 545, 731, 1827, or 3653 days.' Alternatively: 'Choose from predefined retention periods between 1 day and 3653 days (10 years) — see the API reference for the full list.'

issues.statement: Stacktape supports two authorizer types — Cognito (JWT-based) and Lambda (custom logic). Unauthorized requests are rejected with `401 Unauthorized` before your function code runs.
issues.reason: The `StpAuthorizer` type referenced by `HttpApiIntegrationProps.authorizer` is not included in the provided source files, so the specific authorizer variants (Cognito and Lambda) cannot be verified. The claim is plausible based on AWS API Gateway V2 capabilities, but it's a Stacktape-specific assertion about what Stacktape exposes. The draft appropriately defers to the HTTP triggers page for the full API, which limits the risk.
issues.suggestedFix: No change needed if the claim is accurate — just noting it could not be verified from the provided sources. If uncertain, soften to 'See HTTP triggers for supported authorizer types.'

evidence.quote: authorizer?: StpAuthorizer;

positiveFindings: All HttpApiGatewayProps properties (payloadFormat, cors, logging, customDomains, cdn, alarms, disabledGlobalAlarms) are covered in the draft with correct property names matching the source types.

CORS defaults are accurately stated: enabled defaults to false, allowedOrigins defaults to ['*'], and allowedMethods are auto-detected from integrations — all matching the JSDoc @default annotations in HttpApiCorsConfig.

Both alarm trigger types (http-api-gateway-error-rate, http-api-gateway-latency) from the HttpApiGatewayAlarmTrigger union are documented with correct property names (thresholdPercent, thresholdMilliseconds, statistic) and accurate defaults (statistic: 'avg', comparisonOperator: 'GreaterThanThreshold').
Log format options (CLF, JSON, XML, CSV) and the default (JSON) match the source exactly. The disabled property default of false correctly supports the claim that logging is enabled by default.

The ApiReference uses definitionName='HttpApiGatewayProps' which correctly matches the Props-suffixed type convention. ReferenceableParams uses resource='http-api-gateway' matching the resource type string.
All cross-links point to valid routes in the navigation index — no invented URLs detected.

Code examples use valid Stacktape class names (HttpApiGateway, LambdaFunction, StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging, HttpApiIntegration) with proper CodeBlock intellisense format and TypeScript tab labels.

The routing section correctly describes that routes are defined on compute resources via HttpApiIntegration events, not on the gateway itself — consistent with HttpApiGatewayProps having no route definitions.
'When NOT to use' is correctly rendered as a peer H2 section, not nested under 'When to use'.

The comparison table accurately reflects source-grounded differences: HTTP API Gateway integrations support only method+path (per HttpApiIntegrationProps), while ALB supports paths, methods, hosts, headers, queryParams, and sourceIps (per ApplicationLoadBalancerIntegrationProps).

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the provided HTTP API Gateway types, especially around core properties, route integration shape, CORS, custom domains, CDN attachment, logging options, referenceable parameters, and alarm trigger types. The main blockers are a few Stacktape-specific claims that are not supported by the provided source or overstate what the types say.

issues.statement: “Built-in JWT auth” in the comparison table and “Stacktape supports two authorizer types — Cognito (JWT-based) and Lambda (custom logic).”
issues.reason: The provided source only says an HTTP API integration can have an `authorizer?: StpAuthorizer`; it does not define the authorizer variants in the supplied files. The draft makes concrete Stacktape-specific claims about Cognito, JWT, and Lambda authorizers that are not grounded in the provided source set.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to the sourced fact: each HTTP API integration can optionally define an authorizer, and unauthorized requests are rejected with `401 Unauthorized`. Link readers to the HTTP triggers page for authorizer details without naming unsupported variants unless the authorizer type source is provided.

evidence.quote: authorizer?: StpAuthorizer;

#### An authorizer to protect this route.
   *

   *
   * Unauthorized requests will be rejected with a `401 Unauthorized` response.

issues.statement: “Routes are matched by specificity — exact paths take priority over parameterized or greedy paths.”
issues.reason: The source states that exact paths take priority over wildcard paths. It does not mention parameterized routes in the specificity rule, so the draft expands the documented behavior beyond the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the source wording: “Routes are matched by specificity — exact paths take priority over wildcard paths.” Keep parameter and greedy path examples separate from the precedence rule.

evidence.quote: Routes are matched by specificity — exact paths take priority over wildcard paths.

issues.statement: “After deploying, Stacktape prints the gateway URL (e.g., `https://abc123.execute-api.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com`).”
issues.reason: The provided source confirms that `url` is a referenceable parameter, but it does not state that Stacktape automatically prints the HTTP API Gateway URL after deploy. Stack output printing is only described for explicitly configured `stackConfig.outputs`, not automatic resource URL printing.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with the grounded version: “You can reference the deployed gateway URL with `$ResourceParam('myApi', 'url')` or inspect it through the referenceable parameters for the resource.” If you want to mention printed deploy outputs, show an explicit `stackConfig.outputs` example or omit the claim.

issues.statement: “If you manage DNS outside Route53 (e.g., Cloudflare), set `disableDnsRecordCreation: true` and point your DNS manually to the API Gateway domain name.”
issues.reason: The provided source supports skipping DNS record creation for externally managed DNS, but it does not state what target value users should point their external DNS to for an HTTP API Gateway custom domain. The “API Gateway domain name” part is unsupported by the source set.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the sourced part and remove or soften the target instruction: “If you manage DNS outside Route53, set `disableDnsRecordCreation: true`; Stacktape will still provision the TLS certificate but will not create the DNS record.”

evidence.quote:

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Answer-first paragraph is self-contained, fact-dense, and exactly the right length for AI extraction — mentions the AWS service (API Gateway V2), pricing (~$1/million), and key properties without padding.

The FeatureComparisonTable for HTTP API Gateway vs Application Load Balancer is the single most citation-worthy element on the page — covers pricing model, scaling, routing richness, and route-level authorization in one scannable block.
Routing section combines a clear path-pattern table (exact, parameter, greedy) with a working catch-all example and a per-type explanation — exactly the depth the target reader needs.

CDN section meets the decision-support bar: explicit when-to-enable scenario (cacheable public data, geographically distributed users), explicit when-to-skip (fully dynamic/authenticated APIs), cost signal (CloudFront request + data-transfer charges), and an opinionated recommendation.

Payload format section has a comparison table (1.0 vs 2.0), explains each row, and names a clear default recommendation for new projects — all three variant-depth criteria are met for a two-option union.

Alarms section documents both available alarm types (http-api-gateway-error-rate and http-api-gateway-latency), explains every tunable property (statistic, comparisonOperator, evaluation), correctly notes error-rate lacks statistic per the source type, and includes concrete threshold values in the example.

FAQ is exactly 10 questions spanning all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (CORS how-to, custom domain, route add), AWS use-case (cost model, payload limit, cold-start latency), and choosing/comparison (HTTP vs REST API, Gateway vs ALB, WebSocket alternatives).
No forbidden style violations detected: no YAML config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type pattern, no fenced TypeScript blocks containing defineConfig, and no hand-authored YAML tabs.

problems: Route authorization subsection is the page's weakest section — it names that authorizers exist and that unauthorized requests get a 401, but does not name the supported authorizer types (JWT, Lambda authorizer, IAM). A reader deciding whether to add auth has no idea what options are available without following the external link. This is a completeness gap for a meaningful opt-in feature.

The CORS example code sets allowCredentials: true and maxAge: 86400 with accompanying prose explanation, which is good, but the example also includes allowedOrigins: [...] without the surrounding prose explaining that allowedOrigins defaults to ['*'] when omitted — a reader may not realize the default already covers permissive cases without specifying origins.

The FAQ answer for WAF (#9) states 'AWS WAF does not support API Gateway V2 HTTP APIs natively' — this is a well-known AWS constraint but is not grounded in the provided source files. The source only shows that the cdn property is the path to useFirewall; the claim about native WAF unsupport is accurate AWS behavior but should be phrased as 'the Stacktape HttpApiGateway resource does not expose a useFirewall property directly' rather than making a broad AWS product claim that could drift.
optionalImprovements: Route authorization subsection: add one sentence listing the supported authorizer types (e.g., JWT, Lambda authorizer, IAM) before the external link — this is the minimum a reader needs to decide whether auth fits their use case without leaving the page.

Expand the CORS opening sentence to note that 'allowedOrigins defaults to ["*"] when omitted, so you only need to list specific origins if you set allowCredentials: true or want to restrict callers' — this anchors the example value rather than leaving it as magic.

In the FAQ WAF answer, rewrite the claim from a broad AWS product statement to a Stacktape-scoped one: 'The HttpApiGateway resource does not expose a useFirewall property directly; attach a CDN to the gateway and set useFirewall on the CDN configuration to add WAF coverage at the edge.' This is equally accurate, narrowly scoped, and source-grounded.

Consider adding a one-line note in the Basic example prose about what the auto-generated gateway URL looks like (e.g., 'https://{id}.execute-api.{region}.amazonaws.com/') so readers know what they get before they add a custom domain.

The access logging section mentions log forwarding via logForwarding but does not name a single supported destination — adding 'Datadog, Highlight.io, or any HTTP endpoint' in the same sentence as the link would help readers recognize if this is relevant to them without clicking away.

The catch-all pattern subsection says 'This is the most common pattern for building APIs with Stacktape' — this is an opinionated claim that would be strengthened by a brief qualifier like 'when using a full-stack framework such as Hono or Express' to help readers who are not using a framework understand when they should instead use individual route integrations.
